---
title: "Graviton and Kubernetes Right-Sizing: The 40% Infrastructure Margin"
---

<!-- Generated by transform-chapter.ts with openai-compatible/MiniMax-M2 -->
<!-- Density: full | Word target: 1800-2500 -->

Worldwide public cloud spending reached $723.4 billion in 2023, growing 21.5% year-over-year (Flosum). Yet this accelerating investment masks a persistent problem: Future Processing found that 75% of cloud migrations exceed their original budgets, with over a quarter experiencing cost overruns exceeding 20%. Across the industry, unplanned cloud expenditures total approximately $100 billion globally over three years—a margin that most organizations fail to capture.

This chapter addresses a counterintuitive opportunity: the intersection of ARM-based instance migration and Kubernetes right-sizing represents an underutilized margin for reducing cloud spend by 30-50% without requiring refactoring or major architectural changes. ARM-based instance migration (AWS Graviton, Azure ARM, GCP T2A) delivers 20-40% compute cost reduction with low implementation effort. Simultaneously, Kubernetes right-sizing—aligning resource requests with actual utilization—delivers 30-50% cluster cost reduction with medium effort. The combined effect of both optimizations can yield a 40% total infrastructure margin (verified optimization patterns). Organizations without proper visibility into resource utilization cannot identify or capture these savings.

The methodology presented here provides a structured approach to capturing this margin. It begins with automated workload compatibility assessment for ARM migration, proceeds through Kubernetes resource request analysis, and concludes with continuous spend tracking across source and target environments. zopcloud provides migration planning tools with automated cost modeling, workload assessment, and spend tracking to accelerate this process.

## The ARM Instance Migration Opportunity

The ARM-based instance landscape has matured significantly across all three major cloud providers, making this optimization pattern accessible to enterprises regardless of their cloud strategy. AWS offers Graviton2 and Graviton3 processors across over 500 instance types, while Azure provides the Dpsv5 and Dplsv5 series, and GCP delivers the T2A instance family. This shift represents a fundamental architectural transition from x86_64 to ARM64, leveraging the reduced instruction set architecture that powers mobile devices and increasingly powers cloud infrastructure.

The performance advantage stems from ARM's design philosophy: higher core counts at lower clock speeds translate to 20-40% compute cost reduction with low implementation effort (verified optimization patterns). For organizations running containerized workloads, this architectural shift requires minimal refactoring compared to traditional replatforming projects.

Kubernetes support for ARM has reached production maturity across all major managed Kubernetes services. EKS, AKS, and GKE all support ARM node pools, enabling organizations to provision Graviton-based nodes through native cloud provider integrations. A typical node pool configuration specifies the ARM instance type and required capacity:

```yaml
apiVersion: eksctl.io/v1alpha5
kind: ClusterConfig
nodeGroups:
  - name: graviton-workers
    instanceType: t4g.medium
    desiredCapacity: 10
    labels:
      workload-type: containerized
    tags:
      Environment: production
```

Containerized applications built on standard language runtimes such as Python, Node.js, and Go recompile cleanly for ARM64 without source code modifications. The primary validation requirements involve confirming that application dependencies include ARM-compatible container images and that any native libraries have ARM64 builds available. Organizations without proper visibility into resource utilization cannot identify which workloads qualify for this migration path.

For automated compatibility scanning, organizations can evaluate their container image repositories to identify architecture-specific dependencies:

```python
import subprocess
import json
from typing import Dict, List

def check_arm_compatibility(image: str) -> Dict[str, bool]:
    """Check if container image supports ARM64 architecture."""
    try:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["crane", "manifest", image],
            capture_output=True,
            text=True,
            check=True
        )
        manifest = json.loads(result.stdout)
        architectures = manifest.get("manifests", [])
        arm64_supported = any(
            arch.get("platform", {}).get("architecture") == "arm64"
            for arch in architectures
        )
        return {"image": image, "arm64_compatible": arm64_supported}
    except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e:
        return {"image": image, "error": str(e), "arm64_compatible": False}

def scan_repository(org: str) -> List[Dict]:
    """Scan all images in a container repository for ARM compatibility."""
    images = subprocess.run(
        ["gcloud", "container", "images", "list", f"--repository={org}"],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
        check=True
    ).stdout.splitlines()
    
    results = []
    for image in images:
        if image and not image.startswith("Listing"):
            results.append(check_arm_compatibility(image.strip()))
    return results
```

This approach reduces the manual effort required for workload assessment while providing concrete data on migration readiness. Once ARM compatibility is confirmed, organizations can proceed to the Kubernetes right-sizing analysis to capture the full 40% infrastructure margin available through combined optimization patterns.

## Workload Compatibility Assessment for ARM

## Workload Compatibility Assessment for ARM

Before capturing the 20-40% compute cost reduction available through ARM migration (verified optimization patterns), organizations must systematically evaluate workload readiness. A structured compatibility assessment prevents the budget overruns that plague 75% of cloud migrations (Flosum).

**Application Architecture Analysis** begins with cataloging compiled dependencies and native libraries. Containerized applications using Python, Node.js, or Go recompile for ARM64 without source modifications, but applications depending on x86_64 native binaries require replacement or emulation. The assessment must identify every container image, native library, and language runtime across the target workload inventory.

**Build System Compatibility** determines whether existing CI/CD pipelines can produce ARM64 binaries. Organizations should verify that Docker buildx supports multi-architecture builds and that compilation toolchains include ARM64 cross-compilation targets. A practical validation approach:

```python
import subprocess
import yaml

def assess_build_compatibility(dockerfile_path: str) -> dict:
    """Determine if Dockerfile supports ARM64 builds."""
    with open(dockerfile_path) as f:
        content = f.read()
    
    has_multi_stage = "FROM" in content
    has_buildx = "docker buildx" in content or "--platform" in content
    
    return {
        "multi_stage": has_multi_stage,
        "multi_arch_ready": has_buildx,
        "requires_modification": not has_buildx
    }

def generate_arm_build_command(dockerfile: str, image: str) -> str:
    return f"docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t {image} -f {dockerfile} ."
```

**Third-Party Dependencies** require cataloging every commercial and open-source component. Teams must verify ARM64 support for databases, message queues, and monitoring agents before migration planning proceeds.

**Performance Testing Methodology** establishes baseline metrics and acceptance criteria. Define CPU throughput, memory latency, and network bandwidth thresholds that the ARM target must meet or exceed.

The **Pet vs Cattle** scoring system prioritizes migration sequence: stateless APIs and batch processing score highest (5 points), followed by development environments (4 points), stateless microservices (3 points), stateful databases (2 points), and tier-1 production databases (1 point). Organizations should migrate scores of 4-5 first, capturing early savings while building confidence for more complex migrations.

zopcloud provides automated workload assessment that accelerates this compatibility evaluation, scanning container registries and generating migration readiness reports without manual inventorying.

```{.d2 width="100%" file="diagrams/data-pipeline.d2"}
```

*Show the workload assessment workflow for determining ARM readiness*

## Graviton Migration Patterns and Configuration

## Graviton Migration Patterns and Configuration

Deploying ARM-based node pools across EKS, AKS, and GKE requires cloud-specific configurations that leverage each provider's native ARM offerings. AWS Graviton, Azure ARM, and GCP T2A instances integrate directly with managed Kubernetes services through node pool APIs.

EKS node groups specify graviton instance types through the instanceType field, with t3.medium equivalent to t4g.medium providing the entry point for ARM workloads:

```yaml
nodeGroups:
  - name: graviton-pool
    instanceType: r7g.medium
    desiredCapacity: 5
    minSize: 2
    maxSize: 20
    labels:
      kubernetes.io/arch: arm64
      workload-tier: compute
    taints:
      - key: workload-type
        value: arm-native
        effect: NoSchedule
```

AKS node pools use the nodePoolProfile with Dpsv5-series VMs, while GKE specifies the T2A machine family. Workload placement relies on nodeSelector matching the kubernetes.io/arch label, combined with tolerations for tainted ARM pools.

A canary deployment routes a percentage of traffic to ARM pods by scheduling them on dedicated node pools:

```yaml
affinity:
  nodeAffinity:
    requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
            - key: kubernetes.io/arch
              operator: In
              values: ["arm64"]
```

Cluster autoscaler configuration must define expansion policies for both x86 and ARM node pools. Setting expandability-type: priority with higher values for ARM pools ensures cost-optimized nodes provision first, capturing the 20-40% compute cost reduction (verified optimization patterns) while maintaining capacity for x86-constrained workloads.

zopcloud provides migration planning tools with automated cost modeling that tracks spend across source and target environments during this transition.

## Kubernetes Right-Sizing Fundamentals

The Kubernetes scheduler treats resource requests as hard commitments rather than aspirational targets. When a pod defines cpu: "2000m" and memory: "4Gi", Kubernetes guarantees those amounts will be available on some node in the cluster. The scheduler never examines actual consumption—it only sees what the pod requests. This fundamental design choice creates the "utilization gap": the delta between requested resources and what the workload actually consumes at runtime.

In practice, this gap proves substantial. Fear of the OOMKilled status drives this behavior—when Kubernetes cannot terminate a pod gracefully due to memory pressure, it evicts the container immediately, potentially corrupting data or disrupting transactions. This legitimate concern compels teams to request 2x or 3x their typical consumption, accepting wasted cluster capacity as insurance against reliability incidents.

The financial consequences compound across large deployments. Kubernetes right-sizing delivers 30-50% cluster cost reduction with medium effort (verified optimization patterns), yet organizations without proper visibility into resource utilization cannot identify or capture these savings (Flosum). A cluster running 200 pods with average utilization below 40% of requested CPU represents pure waste—every node provisioned to satisfy requests that never materialize.

Three pillars enable effective right-sizing without compromising reliability. First, visibility requires continuous metrics collection through Prometheus, Datadog, or cloud-native monitoring:

```python
from prometheus_client import CollectorRegistry, Gauge, push_to_gateway

def report_resource_utilization(pod_name: str, cpu_request: float, 
                                 cpu_usage: float, memory_request: int,
                                 memory_usage: int):
    """Export utilization metrics for right-sizing analysis."""
    registry = CollectorRegistry()
    cpu_utilization = Gauge(
        'pod_cpu_utilization_ratio', 
        'Actual CPU usage divided by request',
        ['pod', 'namespace'],
        registry=registry
    )
    memory_utilization = Gauge(
        'pod_memory_utilization_ratio',
        'Actual memory usage divided by request', 
        ['pod', 'namespace'],
        registry=registry
    )
    
    cpu_utilization.labels(pod=pod_name, namespace='default').set(
        cpu_usage / cpu_request if cpu_request > 0 else 0
    )
    memory_utilization.labels(pod=pod_name, namespace='default').set(
        memory_usage / memory_request if memory_request > 0 else 0
    )
    
    push_to_gateway('http://prometheus-pushgateway:9091', 
                    job='resource_utilization', registry=registry)
```

Second, analysis compares percentiles (P95, P99) against requests, identifying workloads with consistent headroom. Third, adjustment applies changes iteratively—modifying requests incrementally while monitoring for OOM events and throttling. This is not a one-time project but an operational discipline, as workload behavior evolves with traffic patterns, code deployments, and seasonal peaks.

```{.d2 width="100%" file="diagrams/before-after-optimization.d2"}
```

*Visualize resource request vs actual utilization gap and post-optimization state*

## Container Right-Sizing Methodology

The foundation of container right-sizing rests on collecting representative resource consumption data across your workload's complete operational cycle. Begin by establishing a baseline using kubectl top pods, which provides instant visibility into current CPU and memory usage:

```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace production --sort-by=cpu
kubectl top pods --namespace production --sort-by=memory
```

For historical analysis extending beyond the immediate present, Prometheus queries offer deeper insight. The following query extracts P95 CPU usage over a 14-day window for a specific pod:

```python
from prometheus_api_client import PrometheusConnect

def get_p95_cpu(pod_name: str, namespace: str = "production", 
                days: int = 14) -> float:
    """Query Prometheus for P95 CPU utilization over analysis period."""
    prom = PrometheusConnect(url="http://prometheus:9090")
    query = f'''
    quantile_over_time(
        0.95,
        rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{{
            pod="{pod_name}",
            namespace="{namespace}"
        }}[5m])[{days}d:]
    )
    '''
    result = prom.query(query)
    return float(result[0]['value'][1]) if result else 0.0

# Correlate with application logs for context
def get_log_context_for_oom(pod_name: str, namespace: str):
    """Fetch recent OOM-related logs for correlation."""
    import kubernetes
    v1 = kubernetes.client.CoreV1Api()
    logs = v1.read_namespaced_pod_log(
        name=pod_name,
        namespace=namespace,
        tail_lines=100,
        container='application'
    )
    return [line for line in logs.split('\n') 
            if 'OOM' in line or 'memory' in line.lower()]
```

Analysis period selection directly determines accuracy. A 7-day minimum captures weekly traffic cycles, but 14 days proves essential for workloads with batch jobs, cron triggers, or seasonal peaks. Kubernetes right-sizing delivers 30-50% cluster cost reduction with medium effort, yet organizations without proper visibility into resource utilization cannot identify or capture these savings.

Percentile selection matters more than averages. P95 excludes the top 5% of outliers while capturing genuine spikes—superior to mean calculations that smooth away real demand. Query P95 memory with: quantile_over_time(0.95, container_memory_working_set_bytes[14d]).

Apply safety margins conservatively: 10-15% above P95 for stable production services, 20-25% for workloads with variable traffic patterns. Non-production environments warrant leaner requests—up to 70% reduction in non-production compute costs is achievable by accepting occasional throttling in dev/test clusters.

Iterate adjustments incrementally. Modify requests for one deployment, monitor for OOMKilled events over 48-72 hours, then correlate with application latency metrics. Always maintain rollback capability through deployment history or horizontal pod autoscaler bounds. This cycle repeats continuously as workloads evolve.

## Cost Modeling: Building the Business Case

The hidden 40% margin between your cloud bill and actual infrastructure needs emerges only through rigorous cost modeling before migration begins. Without upfront analysis, organizations discover savings potential only after implementation—when budget conversations become significantly harder. This aligns with the critical insight that 75% of cloud migrations exceed budgets, but ARM migration and Kubernetes right-sizing are lower-risk paths with minimal parallel run requirements.

Building the business case requires three inputs: your current monthly cloud spend, the percentage running on Kubernetes, and your average container request-to-usage ratio. From these, two optimization tracks yield predictable returns. ARM-based instance migration (AWS Graviton, Azure ARM, GCP T2A) delivers 20-40% compute cost reduction with low implementation effort, while Kubernetes right-sizing delivers 30-50% cluster cost reduction with medium effort.

```python
class CostOptimizationCalculator:
    def __init__(self, monthly_cloud_spend: float, 
                 k8s_workload_percentage: float,
                 avg_request_to_usage_ratio: float,
                 on_demand_percentage: float = 1.0):
        self.monthly_spend = monthly_cloud_spend
        self.k8s_pct = k8s_workload_percentage / 100.0
        self.utilization = 1.0 / avg_request_to_usage_ratio if avg_request_to_usage_ratio > 0 else 1.0
        self.on_demand_pct = on_demand_percentage / 100.0
    
    def arm_savings(self, arm_migration_pct: float = 0.30) -> dict:
        """Calculate ARM migration savings at given migration percentage."""
        compute_spend = self.monthly_spend * 0.70  # Assume 70% compute
        migrated_spend = compute_spend * arm_migration_pct
        monthly_savings = migrated_spend * 0.30  # 30% average ARM reduction
        return {
            "monthly_savings": monthly_savings,
            "annual_savings": monthly_savings * 12,
            "implementation_effort": "Low"
        }
    
    def k8s_rightsizing_savings(self, target_reduction: float = 0.40) -> dict:
        """Calculate Kubernetes right-sizing savings."""
        k8s_spend = self.monthly_spend * self.k8s_pct
        waste_factor = 1 - self.utilization
        monthly_savings = k8s_spend * waste_factor * target_reduction
        return {
            "monthly_savings": monthly_savings,
            "annual_savings": monthly_savings * 12,
            "implementation_effort": "Medium"
        }
    
    def combined_annual_savings(self) -> float:
        """Total combined savings from both optimizations."""
        arm = self.arm_savings()["annual_savings"]
        k8s = self.k8s_rightsizing_savings()["annual_savings"]
        return arm + k8s

# Example: $150,000/month cloud spend, 60% Kubernetes, 2.5x request ratio
calculator = CostOptimizationCalculator(
    monthly_cloud_spend=150000,
    k8s_workload_percentage=60,
    avg_request_to_usage_ratio=2.5,
    on_demand_percentage=80
)
```

For spreadsheet models, apply these formulas directly. ARM savings equals monthly compute spend multiplied by ARM migration percentage and then by 0.30. Kubernetes savings equals monthly Kubernetes spend multiplied by the inverse of your average utilization rate and then by 0.40. The financial-services customer achieved $278,000 annual savings ($890,000 → $612,000) through precisely this approach, migrating 340 workloads from on-prem to AWS in 14 weeks.

Implementation timelines follow a predictable cadence. ARM migration begins with workload compatibility assessment (weeks 1-2), progresses through pilot migration (weeks 3-5), and completes with fleet rollout (weeks 6-10). Kubernetes right-sizing requires an initial 14-day metrics baseline, followed by incremental request adjustments across 3-4 weeks, with ongoing optimization thereafter. Non-production environments can realize savings faster—up to 70% reduction in non-production compute costs is achievable within the first month by implementing aggressive right-sizing on dev/test clusters where occasional throttling carries minimal business risk.

::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive Calculator
Adjust the inputs below to model your scenario. Static table shown in PDF/EPUB.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Interactive Calculator
This calculator lets you compare costs across different options.
Adjust the inputs below to see how costs change for your specific scenario.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// --- Input Controls ---

viewof monthlyHours = Inputs.range([100, 10000], {
  value: 2000,
  step: 100,
  label: "Monthly compute hours"
})

viewof storageGB = Inputs.range([50, 5000], {
  value: 500,
  step: 50,
  label: "Storage (GB)"
})

viewof transferGB = Inputs.range([10, 2000], {
  value: 200,
  step: 10,
  label: "Data transfer (GB/month)"
})

viewof region = Inputs.select(
  ["us-east-1", "us-west-2", "eu-west-1", "ap-southeast-1"],
  { value: "us-east-1", label: "Region" }
)
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// --- Cost Models ---
// Customize these rate tables for your comparison scenario.

regionMultiplier = ({
  "us-east-1": 1.0,
  "us-west-2": 1.02,
  "eu-west-1": 1.10,
  "ap-southeast-1": 1.15
})[region]

optionA = {
  const compute = monthlyHours * 0.085 * regionMultiplier;
  const storage = storageGB * 0.023;
  const transfer = transferGB * 0.09;
  const total = compute + storage + transfer;
  return { name: "Option A: On-Demand", compute, storage, transfer, total };
}

optionB = {
  const compute = monthlyHours * 0.053 * regionMultiplier;
  const storage = storageGB * 0.021;
  const transfer = transferGB * 0.085;
  const total = compute + storage + transfer;
  return { name: "Option B: Reserved (1yr)", compute, storage, transfer, total };
}

optionC = {
  const compute = monthlyHours * 0.035 * regionMultiplier;
  const storage = storageGB * 0.018;
  const transfer = transferGB * 0.08;
  const total = compute + storage + transfer;
  return { name: "Option C: Reserved (3yr)", compute, storage, transfer, total };
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// --- Results Table ---

fmt = d3.format("$,.2f")

comparison = [optionA, optionB, optionC]

html`<div class="ojs-calculator">
  <table class="ojs-results-table">
    <thead>
      <tr>
        <th>Category</th>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<th>${o.name}</th>`)}
      </tr>
    </thead>
    <tbody>
      <tr>
        <td>Compute</td>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<td>${fmt(o.compute)}</td>`)}
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td>Storage</td>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<td>${fmt(o.storage)}</td>`)}
      </tr>
      <tr>
        <td>Transfer</td>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<td>${fmt(o.transfer)}</td>`)}
      </tr>
      <tr class="ojs-total-row">
        <td><strong>Monthly Total</strong></td>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<td><strong>${fmt(o.total)}</strong></td>`)}
      </tr>
      <tr class="ojs-total-row">
        <td><strong>Annual Total</strong></td>
        ${comparison.map(o => html`<td><strong>${fmt(o.total * 12)}</strong></td>`)}
      </tr>
    </tbody>
  </table>
  <p class="ojs-savings">
    <strong>Best option:</strong> ${comparison.reduce((a, b) => a.total < b.total ? a : b).name}
    — saves ${fmt((comparison.reduce((a, b) => a.total > b.total ? a : b).total - comparison.reduce((a, b) => a.total < b.total ? a : b).total) * 12)}/year
    vs. the most expensive option.
  </p>
</div>`
```

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
**Cost Comparison (Default Scenario)**

| Category | On-Demand | Reserved 1yr | Reserved 3yr |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Compute  | $170.00   | $106.00     | $70.00      |
| Storage  | $11.50    | $10.50      | $9.00       |
| Transfer | $18.00    | $17.00      | $16.00      |
| **Monthly Total** | **$199.50** | **$133.50** | **$95.00** |
| **Annual Total**  | **$2,394**  | **$1,602**  | **$1,140** |

*Based on 2,000 compute hours, 500 GB storage, 200 GB transfer in us-east-1.
Adjust values in the interactive HTML version.*
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="epub"}
**Cost Comparison (Default Scenario)**

| Category | On-Demand | Reserved 1yr | Reserved 3yr |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Compute  | $170.00   | $106.00     | $70.00      |
| Storage  | $11.50    | $10.50      | $9.00       |
| Transfer | $18.00    | $17.00      | $16.00      |
| **Monthly Total** | **$199.50** | **$133.50** | **$95.00** |
| **Annual Total**  | **$2,394**  | **$1,602**  | **$1,140** |

*Based on 2,000 compute hours, 500 GB storage, 200 GB transfer in us-east-1.
Adjust values in the interactive HTML version.*
:::

## Implementation Roadmap and Timeline

## Implementation Roadmap and Timeline

Executing a combined ARM migration and Kubernetes right-sizing strategy requires structured phasing to manage risk while accelerating time-to-value. Planning discipline directly addresses the reality that many infrastructure projects exceed their original timelines significantly.

**Phase 1 (Weeks 1–2): Workload Assessment and Selection.** Identify ARM-compatible workloads by scanning container images for x86-specific dependencies. Analyze Kubernetes namespaces to surface those with the highest request-to-usage ratios—these yield the fastest Kubernetes right-sizing returns. The financial-services customer completed assessment for 340 workloads in the first two weeks before committing to migration.

**Phase 2 (Weeks 3–4): Infrastructure Preparation.** Provision ARM node pools alongside existing infrastructure, establish Prometheus-based monitoring for both architectures, and define rollback procedures. Configure cost tracking tags to attribute spend accurately during the transition period.

**Phase 3 (Weeks 5–8): Staged Migration.** Execute canary deployments using Kubernetes deployment strategies. Validate performance against baseline metrics before shifting production traffic. The retail-ecommerce customer migrated 180 workloads in 9 weeks using this staged approach.

**Phase 4 (Weeks 9–12): Kubernetes Right-Sizing.** Apply P95-based request adjustments iteratively. Monitor for OOMKilled events and latency regressions during the adjustment cycle.

**Phase 5 (Ongoing): Continuous Optimization.** Conduct monthly reviews to assess newly deployed workloads.

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# ARM compatibility assessment script

SCAN_NAMESPACE="${1:-production}"
OUTPUT_FILE="arm_compatibility_report.json"

echo "Scanning namespace: $SCAN_NAMESPACE for ARM compatibility..."

# Get all deployments in namespace
deployments=$(kubectl get deployments -n "$SCAN_NAMESPACE" -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}')

for deploy in $deployments; do
  # Check image architecture
  image=$(kubectl get deployment "$deploy" -n "$SCAN_NAMESPACE" -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}')
  
  # Query container registry for architecture manifest
  arch=$(curl -s "https://registry.hub.docker.com/v2/repositories/${image}/manifests/latest" 2>/dev/null | \
         jq -r '.schema2 // .manifest // .architecture // "unknown"')
  
  echo "{\"deployment\": \"$deploy\", \"image\": \"$image\", \"architecture\": \"$arch\"}" >> "$OUTPUT_FILE"
done

jq -s '.' "$OUTPUT_FILE"
```

```python
import json
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from kubernetes import client, config

def analyze_namespace_rightsizing_opportunities(namespace: str = "production") -> dict:
    """Analyze Kubernetes namespace for right-sizing opportunities."""
    config.load_kube_config()
    v1 = client.CoreV1Api()
    
    # Fetch all pods in namespace
    pods = v1.list_namespaced_pod(namespace).items
    
    opportunities = []
    
    for pod in pods:
        pod_name = pod.metadata.name
        container = pod.spec.containers[0]
        
        # Extract current requests
        requests = container.resources.requests or {}
        current_cpu = requests.get('cpu', '0')
        current_memory = requests.get('memory', '0')
        
        # Fetch current utilization (requires metrics-server)
        # This requires metrics-server deployed in cluster
        try:
            metrics = v1.read_namespaced_pod_metrics(pod_name, namespace)
            usage = metrics.containers[0].usage
            
            # Calculate request-to-usage ratio
            cpu_request = parse_resource(current_cpu)
            cpu_usage = parse_resource(usage.get('cpu', '0'))
            
            ratio = cpu_request / cpu_usage if cpu_usage > 0 else float('inf')
            
            if ratio > 1.5:
                opportunities.append({
                    "pod": pod_name,
                    "cpu_request": current_cpu,
                    "cpu_usage": usage.get('cpu'),
                    "ratio": round(ratio, 2),
                    "potential_savings_pct": min(40, int((ratio - 1) * 50))
                })
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Skipping {pod_name}: metrics unavailable")
    
    return {
        "namespace": namespace,
        "total_pods": len(pods),
        "opportunities": opportunities,
        "summary": {
            "high_priority_count": len([o for o in opportunities if o['ratio'] > 2.0]),
            "estimated_monthly_savings": sum(o['potential_savings_pct'] for o in opportunities) * 10
        }
    }

def parse_resource(value: str) -> float:
    """Parse Kubernetes resource values (e.g., '500m', '2Gi')."""
    if value.endswith('m'):
        return float(value[:-1]) / 1000
    elif value.endswith('Gi'):
        return float(value[:-2])
    elif value.endswith('Mi'):
        return float(value[:-2]) / 1024
    return float(value)

# Run analysis
results = analyze_namespace_rightsizing_opportunities("production")
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))
```

The phased approach delivers measurable results within 12 weeks while maintaining operational stability. Organizations that skip structured planning frequently encounter timeline overruns—a pattern visible across infrastructure initiatives. Monthly optimization cycles sustain savings as workloads evolve.

## Hidden Costs That Blow Migration Budgets

Budget overruns plague cloud migrations for predictable reasons. The 75% statistic (source: industry migration research) reflects specific, underbudgeted cost categories that derail projects.

**Engineering time** consumes the largest hidden allocation. Compatibility testing for x86-to-ARM workloads typically requires 2-3x the initially estimated engineer-weeks when accounting for dependency resolution, build recompilation, and integration testing. Mitigation involves automated scanning tools that identify problematic libraries before manual review begins.

**Parallel run costs** force organizations to maintain legacy and target environments simultaneously. For a $150,000 monthly infrastructure footprint, dual-running both platforms for 8-12 weeks adds $40,000-$60,000 in pure operational expense. This category alone explains why 75% of migrations exceed budgets.

**Data transfer egress fees** catch teams unprepared. Moving 10TB monthly across regions or clouds incurs $9,000-$15,000 in egress charges depending on provider pricing. Compressing data before transfer and batching transfers during off-peak pricing windows reduces this by 40-60%.

**License recalculations** affect commercial software running on ARM. Per-core licenses with ARM pricing tiers or vendor-specific ARM support contracts introduce cost variables absent from initial quotes. Pre-migration license audits prevent mid-project budget shocks.

**Operational tooling gaps** require new monitoring, logging, and alerting stacks purpose-built for ARM environments. Training existing SREs and platform engineers adds 2-4 weeks of productivity loss per team.

**Opportunity cost**—the projects your best engineers could have delivered—compounds across extended timelines. Each month of parallel running redirects 2-3 senior engineers from revenue-generating work.

These six categories explain the 75% budget overrun rate. ARM migration and Kubernetes right-sizing avoid most pitfalls because they require no parallel runs and minimal application refactoring—delivering 20-40% compute savings with low implementation effort while sidestepping the hidden costs that derail traditional lift-and-shift projects.

## Summary: Capturing the 40% Margin

The combined effect of ARM-based instance migration and Kubernetes resource right-sizing delivers a 40% total infrastructure margin—an efficiency gain that transforms cloud economics without requiring fundamental application refactoring. AWS Graviton, Azure ARM, and GCP T2A migrations achieve 20-40% compute cost reduction with low implementation effort, while Kubernetes right-sizing—aligning resource requests with actual P95 utilization—yields 30-50% cluster cost reduction at medium effort.

These optimizations sidestep the budget overruns that plague 75% of cloud migrations because they require no parallel run periods and minimal application changes. The technical execution follows well-established patterns, but the organizational challenge is visibility: organizations without proper visibility into resource utilization cannot identify or capture these savings. zopcloud provides migration planning tools with automated cost modeling, workload assessment, and spend tracking across source and target environments—surfacing optimization opportunities that manual processes typically miss.

As ARM architecture matures and Kubernetes ecosystems standardize resource management, early adopters will build compounding advantages. Their infrastructure margins will widen as the rest of the industry catches up to what is already achievable today.
